content:
We first observe that the 3×3–grid is a bipartite graph (adjacency is only between “white” and “black” squares in a chess‐board colouring).  In order that every edge join an odd to an even, all 5 squares of one colour must be odd and the 4 of the other colour even.  Since there are 5 odd numbers and 4 even numbers from 1 to 9, the only possibility is:

–  Place the 5 odd numbers in the 5 “white’’ squares in any order,  
–  Place the 4 even numbers in the 4 “black’’ squares in any order.  

Hence the total number of raw Isthmian labellings is  
 5!·4! = 120·24 = 2880.  

We now quotient by the 4‐element rotation group.  By Burnside’s Lemma the number of orbits is

\[
\frac{1}{4}\bigl(N(\mathrm{id})+N(90°)+N(180°)+N(270°)\bigr).
\]

Clearly only the identity fixes any labelling—no nontrivial rotation can carry a labelling with nine distinct numbers to itself.  Thus

N(id)=2880,  N(90°)=N(180°)=N(270°)=0,

and the number of orbits (i.e. the number of arrangements up to rotation) is

\[
\frac{2880+0+0+0}{4} \;=\;720.
\]

Answer: \boxed{720}.
